concepts.name: bayesian neural networks
concepts.description: Neural networks trained using Bayesian inference methods, which emerged as top performers in benchmark comparisons for classification tasks.
concepts.quote: Bayesian neural networks again emerge as the winner, although for some datasets the differences between the test error rates is not statistically significant.

concepts.name: boosted neural networks
concepts.description: Neural networks combined with boosting algorithms to create ensemble models with improved prediction accuracy.
concepts.quote: The superiority of boosted neural networks over boosted trees suggest that the neural network model is better suited to these particular problems.

concepts.name: bagged neural networks
concepts.description: Neural networks combined with bootstrap aggregating (bagging) to create ensemble models.
concepts.quote: The figure and table show Bayesian, boosted and bagged neural networks, boosted trees, and random forests, using both the screened and reduced features sets.

concepts.name: screened features
concepts.description: Features that have been filtered or selected before model training, creating a legitimate self-contained procedure.
concepts.quote: Since the reduced feature sets come from the Bayesian neural network approach, only the methods that use the screened features are legitimate, self-contained procedures.

concepts.name: ard reduced features
concepts.description: Features reduced using Automatic Relevance Determination from Bayesian neural networks, representing a data-driven dimensionality reduction.
concepts.quote: Since the reduced feature sets come from the Bayesian neural network approach

concepts.name: neural network computational complexity
concepts.description: The computational cost of fitting neural networks, typically requiring O(NpML) operations where N is observations, p is predictors, M is hidden units, and L is training epochs.
concepts.quote: With N observations, p predictors, M hidden units and L training epochs, a neural network fit typically requires O(NpML) operations.

concepts.name: optimal separating hyperplane
concepts.description: A hyperplane that maximally separates two linearly separable classes by creating the biggest margin between training points for each class.
concepts.quote: Optimal separating hyperplanes are introduced in Chapter 4 for the case when two classes are linearly separable.

concepts.name: support vector classifier
concepts.description: An extension of optimal separating hyperplanes to nonseparable cases where classes overlap, allowing some points on the wrong side of the margin using slack variables.
concepts.quote: Here we cover extensions to the nonseparable case, where the classes overlap.

concepts.name: margin
concepts.description: The distance M from the separating hyperplane to the closest training point, where M = 1/||β||.
concepts.quote: Hence we are able to find the hyperplane that creates the biggest margin between the training points for class 1 and −1.

concepts.name: slack variables
concepts.description: Variables ξi that allow points to violate the margin constraints in support vector classifiers, measuring the degree of overlap between classes.
concepts.quote: Define the slack variables ξ = (ξ1, ξ2, ..., ξN).

concepts.name: convex optimization
concepts.description: An optimization problem with quadratic criterion and linear inequality constraints that has a unique global solution.
concepts.quote: This is a convex optimization problem (quadratic criterion, linear inequality constraints), and the solution is characterized in Section 4.5.2.

concepts.name: nonconvex optimization
concepts.description: An optimization problem that may have multiple local minima, resulting from certain formulations like measuring overlap in actual distance from margin.
concepts.quote: However, the first choice results in a nonconvex optimization problem, while the second is convex.

concepts.name: kernel methods
concepts.description: Techniques that produce nonlinear decision boundaries by constructing linear boundaries in transformed high-dimensional feature spaces.
concepts.quote: These techniques are then generalized to what is known as the support vector machine, which produces nonlinear boundaries by constructing a linear boundary in a large, transformed version of the feature space.

concepts.name: reproducing kernel hilbert space
concepts.description: A mathematical framework (RKHS) for function estimation with kernels, providing theoretical foundation for support vector machines.
concepts.quote: Function Estimation and Reproducing Kernels

concepts.name: penalization method
concepts.description: A formulation of SVMs as optimization with a penalty term, connecting SVMs to regularization approaches.
concepts.quote: The SVM as a Penalization Method

concepts.name: svm for classification
concepts.description: Support vector machine adapted for classification tasks by finding optimal decision boundaries in feature space.
concepts.quote: Computing the SVM for Classification

concepts.name: linear decision boundary
concepts.description: A hyperplane that separates classes in the original feature space, defined by f(x) = x^T β + β0 = 0.
concepts.quote: In this chapter we describe generalizations of linear decision boundaries for classification.

concepts.name: nonlinear decision boundary
concepts.description: A decision boundary that is not a hyperplane, achieved by SVMs through kernel transformations of the feature space.
concepts.quote: These techniques are then generalized to what is known as the support vector machine, which produces nonlinear boundaries by constructing a linear boundary in a large, transformed version of the feature space.

concepts.name: feature space transformation
concepts.description: Mapping input features to a higher-dimensional space where linear boundaries can represent complex nonlinear patterns.
concepts.quote: which produces nonlinear boundaries by constructing a linear boundary in a large, transformed version of the feature space

concepts.name: signed distance
concepts.description: The distance from a point x to a hyperplane, with sign indicating which side of the boundary, given by f(x) = x^T β + β0.
concepts.quote: f(x) in (12.1) gives the signed distance from a point x to the hyperplane f(x) = xT β+β0 = 0

concepts.name: margin constraint
concepts.description: Constraints ensuring that all training points are at least distance M from the decision boundary: yi*f(xi) >= M for all i.
concepts.quote: we can find a function f(x) = xT β + β0 with yif(xi) > 0 ∀i

concepts.name: overlap penalty
concepts.description: A penalty term Σξi that measures the total amount of overlap when classes are not perfectly separable.
concepts.quote: There are two natural ways to modify the constraints: ∀i, ξi ≥0, Σξi ≤constant.

concepts.name: cost parameter
concepts.description: A tuning parameter C that controls the tradeoff between margin width and training error in support vector classifiers.
concepts.quote: The two choices lead to different solutions.

concepts.name: kernel function
concepts.description: A function K(x, x') that computes inner products in a transformed feature space without explicitly computing the transformation h(x).
concepts.quote: we need not specify the transformation h(x) at all, but require only knowledge of the kernel function

concepts.name: polynomial kernel
concepts.description: A kernel function of the form K(x, x') = (1 + ⟨x, x'⟩)^d that computes polynomial feature expansions efficiently.
concepts.quote: Consider for example a feature space with two inputs X1 and X2, and a polynomial kernel of degree 2.

concepts.name: radial basis kernel
concepts.description: A kernel function K(x, x') = exp(-γ||x - x'||²) that measures similarity based on Euclidean distance, also known as RBF or Gaussian kernel.
concepts.quote: Radial basis: K(x, x′) = exp(−γ∥x −x′∥2)

concepts.name: neural network kernel
concepts.description: A kernel function K(x, x') = tanh(κ1⟨x, x'⟩ + κ2) inspired by neural network activation functions.
concepts.quote: Neural network: K(x, x′) = tanh(κ1⟨x, x′⟩+ κ2).

concepts.name: lagrange multipliers
concepts.description: Optimization variables (αi) used to solve constrained optimization problems by converting them to dual form.
concepts.quote: We describe a quadratic programming solution using Lagrange multipliers.

concepts.name: lagrangian primal function
concepts.description: The original formulation of the optimization problem before conversion to dual form, incorporating constraints via Lagrange multipliers.
concepts.quote: The Lagrange (primal) function is

concepts.name: wolfe dual
concepts.description: The dual objective function obtained by substituting the stationarity conditions into the Lagrangian, which is easier to solve than the primal.
concepts.quote: By substituting (12.10)–(12.12) into (12.9), we obtain the Lagrangian (Wolfe) dual objective function

concepts.name: inner product representation
concepts.description: A formulation where the optimization problem and solution depend on input features only through inner products, enabling the kernel trick.
concepts.quote: We can represent the optimization problem (12.9) and its solution in a special way that only involves the input features via inner products.

concepts.name: basis expansion
concepts.description: Enlarging the feature space using transformations such as polynomials or splines to make linear methods more flexible.
concepts.quote: we can make the procedure more flexible by enlarging the feature space using basis expansions such as polynomials or splines

concepts.name: hinge loss
concepts.description: A loss function L(y, f) = [1 - yf]+ used in SVMs that penalizes points within the margin or on the wrong side.
concepts.quote: Examination of the "hinge" loss function L(y, f) = [1...

concepts.name: loss plus penalty formulation
concepts.description: The reformulation of SVMs as minimizing a loss function (hinge loss) plus a penalty term (regularization), connecting SVMs to other statistical methods.
concepts.quote: This has the form loss + penalty, which is a familiar paradigm in function estimation.

concepts.name: regularization parameter
concepts.description: Parameter λ (equal to 1/C) that controls the tradeoff between model complexity and training error in the loss+penalty formulation.
concepts.quote: It is easy to show (Exercise 12.1) that the solution to (12.25), with λ = 1/C, is the same as that for (12.8).

concepts.name: linearly separable
concepts.description: A property of two classes when there exists a hyperplane that perfectly separates them with no overlap.
concepts.quote: Optimal separating hyperplanes are introduced in Chapter 4 for the case when two classes are linearly separable.

concepts.name: nonseparable case
concepts.description: A situation where two classes overlap in feature space and cannot be perfectly separated by a hyperplane.
concepts.quote: Here we cover extensions to the nonseparable case, where the classes overlap.

concepts.name: svm path algorithm
concepts.description: An efficient algorithm for fitting the entire sequence of SVM models obtained by varying the cost parameter C, similar to LARS for lasso.
concepts.quote: Here we describe a path algorithm (in the spirit of Section 3.8) for efficiently fitting the entire sequence of SVM models obtained by varying C.

concepts.name: multiclass svm
concepts.description: Extension of binary SVM to multiclass problems by building classifiers for each pair of classes and using voting or multinomial loss.
concepts.quote: The support vector machine can be extended to multiclass problems, essentially by solving many two-class problems. A classifier is built for each pair of classes, and the final classifier is the one that dominates the most.

concepts.name: one-versus-one classification
concepts.description: A multiclass strategy where a binary classifier is built for each pair of classes, with the final prediction based on voting.
concepts.quote: A classifier is built for each pair of classes, and the final classifier is the one that dominates the most

concepts.name: overfit boundary
concepts.description: An excessively complex, wiggly decision boundary that fits training data too closely, resulting from large values of the cost parameter C.
concepts.quote: A large value of C will discourage any positive ξi, and lead to an overfit wiggly boundary in the original feature space

concepts.name: smooth boundary
concepts.description: A decision boundary with low complexity achieved by small values of ||β||, controlled by a small cost parameter C.
concepts.quote: a small value of C will encourage a small value of ∥β∥, which in turn causes f(x) and hence the boundary to be smoother.

concepts.name: tuning parameter
concepts.description: A parameter (such as C in SVMs) that controls model complexity and must be selected via methods like cross-validation.
concepts.quote: The tuning parameter of this procedure is the cost parameter C.

concepts.name: projection pursuit regression
concepts.description: A regression method specialized from projection pursuit that finds interesting low-dimensional projections of the data.
concepts.quote: Projection pursuit was proposed by Friedman and Tukey (1974), and specialized to regression by Friedman and Stuetzle (1981).

concepts.name: epsilon-insensitive loss
concepts.description: A loss function for SVM regression that ignores errors smaller than ε, analogous to how SVMs ignore well-classified points far from the boundary.
concepts.quote: This is an "ϵ-insensitive" error measure, ignoring errors of size less than ϵ. There is a rough analogy with the support vector classification setup, where points on the correct side of the decision boundary and far away from it, are ignored in the optimization.

concepts.name: svm for regression
concepts.description: Extension of support vector machines to regression problems using epsilon-insensitive loss to create a tube around the prediction function.
concepts.quote: This is an "ϵ-insensitive" error measure, ignoring errors of size less than ϵ.

concepts.name: smoothing splines
concepts.description: A regression method using spline basis functions with penalty on roughness, related to projection pursuit and regularization methods.
concepts.quote: Roosen and Hastie (1994) present a formulation using smoothing splines.

concepts.name: radial basis function expansion
concepts.description: A basis expansion method using radial basis functions, mathematically equivalent to certain kernel methods and regularization estimates.
concepts.quote: Notice that this has the same form as both the radial basis function expansion and a regularization estimate, discussed in Chapters 5 and 6.

concepts.name: regularization estimate
concepts.description: A statistical estimation method that adds a penalty term to prevent overfitting, mathematically connected to kernel methods and basis expansions.
concepts.quote: Notice that this has the same form as both the radial basis function expansion and a regularization estimate, discussed in Chapters 5 and 6.

concepts.name: training-class separation
concepts.description: The degree to which classes can be separated in a feature space, improved by enlarging the space with basis expansions.
concepts.quote: Generally linear boundaries in the enlarged space achieve better training-class separation

concepts.name: kkt conditions
concepts.description: Karush-Kuhn-Tucker optimality conditions that characterize the solution to constrained optimization problems, implying labeled points fall into distinct groups.
concepts.quote: It can be shown that the KKT optimality conditions imply that the labeled points (xi, yi) fall into three distinct groups

concepts.name: karush-kuhn-tucker conditions
concepts.description: Necessary and sufficient conditions for optimality in constrained optimization, including the primal-dual relationships and complementarity slackness.
concepts.quote: In addition to (12.10)–(12.12), the Karush–Kuhn–Tucker conditions include the constraints

concepts.name: feasible point
concepts.description: A point that satisfies all constraints in an optimization problem, used to establish bounds on the objective function.
concepts.quote: which gives a lower bound on the objective function (12.8) for any feasible point

concepts.name: duality gap
concepts.description: The difference between the primal and dual objective values, which is zero at optimality for convex problems.
concepts.quote: which gives a lower bound on the objective function

concepts.name: linear inequality constraints
concepts.description: Constraints of the form g(x) ≤ 0 in optimization problems, which preserve convexity when combined with a convex objective.
concepts.quote: This is a convex optimization problem (quadratic criterion, linear inequality constraints)

concepts.name: quadratic criterion
concepts.description: An objective function that is quadratic in the optimization variables, ensuring convexity and unique solutions.
concepts.quote: This is a convex optimization problem (quadratic criterion, linear inequality constraints)
